votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately translating the string prefix XOR logic, character conversions, and length constraints.
The Python code accurately implements the Coq specifications, correctly handling the string lengths, character-by-character XOR logic, and invalid character cases.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, accurately translating the string prefix XOR logic and character conversions.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The Coq string_xor_prefix recursively checks that: (1) a and r have the same length (both empty or both non-empty together), (2) b has at least as many characters as a (exists cb b' such that b = String cb b'), and (3) each character pair XORs correctly. The Python version checks len(a) == len(r), len(b) >= len(a), and iterates through verifying xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) == r[i]. Both return the same concrete boolean/truth value for all valid binary string inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The Coq string_xor_prefix recursively checks that: (1) a and r have the same length (both empty or both non-empty together), (2) b has at least as many characters as a (existentially quantified cb, b' means b must have a character at each position), and (3) each character in r equals the XOR of corresponding characters in a and b. The Python implementation checks these same conditions: len(a) == len(r), len(b) >= len(a), and xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) == r[i] for all positions. The helper functions ascii_of_bool, bool_of_ascii, and xor_ascii also match their Coq counterparts for concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The Coq `string_xor_prefix` recursively checks that for each character position, the XOR of corresponding characters from `a` and `b` equals the character in `r`, requiring `a` and `r` to have the same length and `b` to be at least as long as `a`. The Python implementation checks the same conditions: `len(a) == len(r)`, `len(b) >= len(a)`, and for each index `i`, `xor_ascii(a[i], b[i]) == r[i]`. The helper functions `ascii_of_bool`, `bool_of_ascii`, and `xor_ascii` behave identically in both versions for valid binary string characters ('0' and '1').

Python mirrors the Coq spec: it requires a and r to have equal length, allows b to be at least that long, and checks each prefix character so that r[i] is the XOR of a[i] and b[i] with '0'/'1' mapping. Non-binary chars cause failure in both. Extra trailing chars in b are allowed in both. Thus concrete behavior matches.

Python mirrors Coq: it maps only '0'/'1' to booleans, XORs them, and fails otherwise; string_xor_prefix enforces |a|=|r|, |b|>=|a|, and per-position XOR equality, with the empty a/r case returning True regardless of b. This matches the Coq existential prefix spec on all inputs.
Python enforces len(a)=len(r), len(b)>=len(a), and for each position computes XOR only for '0'/'1' chars, failing otherwise—matching the Coq prefix relation and xor_ascii behavior.
